Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients  | Spices and herbs

**Coriander** is a spice from the same plant that produces cilantro, also known as "coriander leaf".

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

The spice takes the form of round, tan seeds—technically seed pods—with a fragrant aroma when crushed.[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) The characteristic aroma is different from that of cilantro, with elements of orange/lemon peel, sage, caraway, and pine.**[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)**[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) The seeds are usually dried but can be eaten green.

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

If the spice is bought whole in a non-dried form, it can be dried in the sun. Most commonly, however, it is bought in its dried form, either whole or ground.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6) Store coriander in a tightly sealed container away from sunlight and heat.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) For maximum flavour, grind only right before use, use within 6 months, and keep for no more than 1 year.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Coriander is commonly used in Latin American, West-, South-, and South-east Asian cooking. The seeds are a key ingredient in Indian curries and garam masala, along with other spice mixes.[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6) It is also used in Ethiopian and Arabic cooking, and it can sometimes flavor baked goods and liqueurs.[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

If working with whole seeds, they can be briefly roasted or heated in a dry pan to enhance the aroma before grinding in an electric grinder or mortar and pestle.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4) It is also recommended to cook the seeds in oil to help release the fat-soluble flavors.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6)

## Substitution
